title: Reporting, Documentation, and Test Completion
narrationScript: Ask a quality manager what an inspection is worth without a record and you will get a short answer. Nothing. In the eyes of the quality system, an examination that is not documented did not happen. This final lesson covers the paper, the pictures, and the discipline that turn good inspection work into a defensible record, plus the checklist habit that confirms a test is actually complete. The outline splits documentation into three layers, and it helps to keep them straight. The technique report records how you looked. The data report records what you found. The image report proves it. The technique report carries the procedure number and revision, whether the technique was direct or remote, every instrument with its serial number and calibration due date, the lighting source and the measured illuminance at the surface, the viewing distance and angle, the surface preparation, any access limitations, and who performed the examination, with certification level. The test of a good technique report is simple. Could another qualified examiner reproduce your examination conditions from the page alone. If yes, it is complete. Record actual measured values, not just a checkmark. If the procedure minimum is one thousand lux for the critical examination, your report should show the number you actually read at the surface. The data report is the indication log. Every indication gets a unique identifier, a location from a named datum, an orientation, an interpretation, its measured dimensions, the criteria paragraph it was evaluated against, and a disposition. Accept, reject, or rework and reexamine. Finding nothing is also data. A clean examination still gets a statement of no recordable indications plus a statement of what was actually covered. Write measured values in consistent units. The word okay is not a measurement. The image report is your objective evidence, sketches and photographs. A sketch needs a datum, orientation arrows, the indication identifiers marked at their positions, key dimensions, and a note that it is not to scale if it is not. A photograph needs three things every single time. A scale reference in the frame. A location or orientation reference. And an identifier that ties it to a line in the data report. A photo without those three is a pretty picture, not evidence. For digital photos, keep the original unedited file, check focus before you leave the site, and make sure the resolution actually resolves the features the criteria care about. Control glare with diffuse light, and shoot an overview plus a close up for every recorded indication, because the indication may be ground out an hour after you photograph it. Then comes completion. Done looking is not the same as done testing. The outline calls for completion of testing to be confirmed with a checklist, and the checklist earns its keep at the end of a long shift, when memory is the least reliable instrument in your kit. Walk it line by line. Was every item in the scope examined, and were any access limitations documented and accepted. Is every indication in the log dispositioned, with no orphans. Were reworked areas reexamined to the original procedure and criteria. Are the technique, data, and image records complete and cross referenced. Were all instruments in calibration, and does the lighting record meet or beat the minimum. Is the criteria document cited with edition and paragraph. Is the report signed and dated by a certified examiner, and are customer witness points cleared. Are the files archived per the retention requirement. When every line checks, and only then, the test is complete. Years from now, a failure investigator, an auditor, or a lawyer may read your report cold, with you nowhere in the room. Write it so a stranger can stand where you stood, see what you saw, and reach the same conclusion. That is the standard your signature promises. In the practice exercise you will sort report content into the right layer, technique, data, or image, until the filing decision is automatic. Get that reflex right and your reports will assemble themselves in the field, complete, consistent, and ready for any reader the future sends.

sections.heading: The Report Is the Product
sections.body:
In the quality system's eyes, an undocumented examination never happened. The report is not paperwork after the job — it **is** the product of the job. It communicates dispositions to the people who act on them, lets another examiner repeat the examination years later, and stands as evidence in audits, warranty disputes, and failure investigations long after everyone involved has changed employers.

CP-105 splits VT documentation into three layers: **technique reports** record how you examined, **data reports** record what you found, and **image reports** prove it with sketches and photographs. A complete record also carries the traceability thread that ties the layers together: part identity, procedure and revision, acceptance criteria document and edition, instruments with calibration status, lighting values, examiner identity and certification level, and date. Break any link in that thread and the record loses its value — a perfect indication log evaluated against an unnamed criteria edition cannot be audited, defended, or repeated.

sections.heading: Technique Reports: How You Looked
sections.body:
The technique report answers one question: could another qualified examiner reproduce your examination conditions from the page alone?

Contents that make the answer yes:

- Procedure number and revision; technique — direct, remote, or translucent.
- Equipment with serial numbers and calibration due dates: light meter, borescope and tip adapters, gauges.
- Lighting: source type and the **measured** illuminance at the examination surface — record the actual reading, such as 1,080 lux against a 1,000 lux minimum, never just a checkmark.
- Viewing geometry: confirmation the eye was within 24 inches of the surface and at not less than 30 degrees to it, or the remote-equivalent demonstration.
- Surface condition and preparation: as-welded, wire-brushed, ground, cleaning method.
- Access limitations and any approved deviations from the procedure.
- Personnel: name and certification level of whoever examined.

Two field habits pay off. First, write the technique block at the start of the examination, while the meter is still in your hand — reconstructed numbers are the first thing an auditor distrusts. Second, when conditions change mid-job — lamp swapped, surface re-prepped, access opened — the change gets its own entry. One technique block per set of conditions, not one per shift.

sections.heading: Data Reports: What You Found
sections.body:
The data report is the indication log — the factual core of the record. Each entry carries:

- A unique indication ID that every photo, sketch, and disposition references.
- Location from a **named datum** — 240 mm from datum B along weld W-12 — never from a movable or ambiguous feature.
- Orientation, interpretation, and type: linear or rounded, crack-like, undercut, porosity.
- Measured dimensions in consistent units: length, width, depth as applicable. Write the number; OK is not a measurement.
- The criteria clause evaluated against, and the disposition: accept, reject, or rework — with reworked areas re-examined and the re-examination logged.

A clean job still produces data: a statement of no recordable indications, plus the coverage actually achieved — which welds, which surfaces, what percentage — and any areas not examined, with reasons. Coverage claims are commitments: claiming 100 percent means you can show every item on the scope list was seen under procedure conditions. If a tube row was blocked, the data report says so, because an unexamined area recorded as clean is the most dangerous document in NDT.

sections.caption: Anatomy of a complete VT report: traceability header, technique block, indication-log data block, image block with referenced sketch and photo, and the sign-off that closes the record.

sections.heading: Image Reports: Sketches and Photographs
sections.body:
Images convert your testimony into objective evidence.

**Sketches.** A good sketch shows the part outline with a datum reference, orientation arrows — north, flow direction, weld-numbering direction — each indication ID at its measured position, the key dimensions to datums, and a not-to-scale note unless it truly is to scale. Cross-reference every ID to its data-report line.

**Photographs.** Compose every recorded-indication photo as a pair: an overview locating the area on the part, and a close-up resolving the indication. In the close-up include a scale reference — a rule or calibrated scale tab in the plane of the indication — and an ID tag or slate. Control glare with diffuse light or by angling the lamp; verify focus on the camera screen before leaving the location, because the indication may be ground out an hour later. For digital photos, retain the original unedited file, confirm the date stamp is correct, and check that the resolution actually resolves the features the criteria govern — a 2 mm porosity limit demands photos in which 1 mm features are clearly distinguishable. Hard-copy prints get labeled and archived per the retention requirement. Where geometry blocks the camera, a surface replica — foil or casting compound — can document fine surface detail instead.

sections.title: Three Things Every Photo Needs
sections.body: A scale reference in the frame, a location or orientation reference, and an identifier tying the image to a specific line of the data report. A photograph missing any of the three is a pretty picture, not objective evidence — and it will not survive its first audit question.

sections.heading: Confirming Completion with a Checklist
sections.body:
Done looking is not done testing. CP-105 closes the VT body of knowledge with the requirement that completion of testing be **confirmed with a checklist** — because at the end of a long shift, memory is the least calibrated instrument you own.

The checklist walks the whole record:

- **Scope** — every item on the work order examined; access limitations documented and accepted by the responsible party.
- **Indications** — every log entry carries a disposition, no orphans; rework areas re-examined to the original procedure and criteria.
- **Records** — technique, data, and image reports complete and cross-referenced by indication ID.
- **Validity** — instrument calibrations current; lighting record at or above the procedure minimum; examiner certification current for the method and level.
- **Authority** — criteria document cited with edition and paragraph; report signed and dated; customer witness and hold points cleared.
- **Archive** — files stored per the retention requirement, originals preserved.

Sign the completion line only when every box is honestly checked. The discipline feels bureaucratic right up until the day a failure investigation, an auditor, or opposing counsel reads your record cold — then it feels like armor.

sections.caption: Example test-completion checklist.
rows: All scope items examined, or exceptions documented
An unexamined area recorded as clean is worse than no record at all
Every indication dispositioned — accept, reject, or rework
Orphan indications resurface in audits and investigations
Rework areas re-examined to the original procedure
Repair without re-examination voids the original acceptance
Illuminance readings recorded and at or above the minimum
Proves the examination could actually see what it claims
Instrument serials and calibration due dates recorded
Out-of-calibration tools invalidate every number they produced
Sketches and photos carry scale, datum, and indication IDs
Images without references are not objective evidence
Criteria document, edition, and paragraph cited
An evaluation cannot be repeated against an unnamed rulebook
Report signed and dated by a certified examiner
An unsigned record carries no certification authority
Files archived per the retention requirement
Records that cannot be retrieved do not exist

figures.title: Anatomy of a Complete VT Report

interactive.title: File It in the Right Report
interactive.intro: A complete VT record has three layers: the technique report records how you examined, the data report records what you found, and the image report proves it. Sort each item into the report where it belongs.

items.label: Procedure number and revision used
items.why: How the examination was performed starts with which procedure governed it — a technique-report element.

items.label: Measured 1,080 lux at the surface, light meter serial 4412
items.why: Lighting conditions and the instrument that verified them document how you examined — technique report.

items.label: Borescope model, tip adapter, and calibration due date
items.why: Equipment identity and calibration status belong with the examination conditions — technique report.

items.label: Indication VT-07: linear, 6 mm, at the weld toe, 240 mm from datum B
items.why: The indication log — IDs, locations, types, sizes — is the heart of the data report.

items.label: Disposition: reject per the cited criteria paragraph
items.why: Evaluation results and dispositions are findings — they live in the data report.

items.label: Statement of no recordable indications, with coverage achieved
items.why: A clean result is still a finding. It is recorded as data along with the coverage statement.

items.label: Weld-map sketch with datum arrow and indication IDs
items.why: Sketches are image-report content, cross-referenced to the data-report IDs they illustrate.

items.label: Close-up digital photo with a scale rule beside the indication
items.why: Photographs carrying scale, orientation, and an ID are the objective-evidence layer — the image report.

items.label: Photo log listing frame numbers and their locations on the part
items.why: The index tying each image to its location keeps the image report auditable — it travels with the images.

checkQuestions.question: Measured illuminance, the light meter serial number, and the surface preparation method belong in which documentation layer?
options: Data report
Image report
Technique report
They are optional notes and need not be recorded
checkQuestions.explanation: Everything describing HOW the examination was performed — procedure, equipment, lighting, surface condition, geometry, personnel — is technique-report content.

checkQuestions.question: An examination finds nothing recordable. What does the data report contain?
options: Nothing — no findings means no report is filed
A statement of no recordable indications, plus the coverage actually examined, the criteria reference, and the required signatures
Only the single word ACCEPT
A general photo of the area with no other entries
checkQuestions.explanation: A clean result is still a finding. The record must state what was covered and to what criteria, or the claim of acceptance cannot be audited.

checkQuestions.question: A close-up photograph of an indication is unusable as objective evidence if it lacks:
options: Heavy editing to enhance the contrast
A scale reference, an orientation or location reference, and an ID linking it to the indication log
A wide-angle view of the entire shop
Conversion to black and white
checkQuestions.explanation: Without scale, location, and a tie to a specific data-report line, a photo proves nothing about size or position — the three references are what make it evidence.

checkQuestions.question: What is the purpose of the completion checklist at the end of a VT job?
options: It replaces the final report when time is short
To confirm coverage, dispositions, and records are complete before the test is declared finished
To document only the rejected items
To transfer responsibility for the findings to the welder
checkQuestions.explanation: CP-105 calls for completion of testing to be confirmed with a checklist: every scope item examined, every indication dispositioned, every record complete and valid before sign-off.

checkQuestions.question: Years later, a failure investigation pulls your VT report. Which set of items makes the examination reproducible?
options: Part ID, procedure and revision, criteria document and edition, instruments with calibration status, measured lighting, examiner identity, and date
Part ID and date only — the rest can be reconstructed from memory
The final disposition only, since that is the conclusion that matters
A general statement that standard practice was followed
checkQuestions.explanation: Reproducibility requires the full traceability thread. Missing links — an unnamed criteria edition, an unrecorded instrument — leave the examination impossible to repeat or defend.

references: CP-105-2024 VT Level II Topical Outline §8.4–8.5
Employer written practice and quality-manual requirements for NDT records (generic)
Documentation practice in codes such as ASME Section V, Article 9 (generic)
